burial.plot_notes: Forest Lawn Cemetery Saginaw per FA — buried in Michigan despite dying in Florida

narrative_blocks.header: Graduated Helmuth College and attended University of Michigan
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline Mary Magoffin Humphrey graduated from Helmuth College in London, Ontario, Canada — an Anglican women's college — before attending the University of Michigan, an unusual educational path for a woman born in Portage, Wisconsin in 1861, at a time when fewer than two percent of American women attended college at all. She married Watts Sherman Humphrey, a Civil War veteran and frontier attorney, in Chicago in January 1888, and their eldest son George Magoffin Humphrey became Eisenhower's Secretary of the Treasury — suggesting that her educational ambition was not lost on the generation she raised.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

career.notes: Significant corporate directorships in industrial Michigan.

military_service.notes: Enlisted December 11, 1863; discharged May 18, 1865. Horse shot under him at Battle of the Wilderness (May 1864); wounded at Battle of Trevilian Station (June 1864). Admitted Harper's Hospital Detroit January 2, 1865.

institutions.institution_blurb: Graduated University of Michigan Law 1869; went on to found Humphrey & Perks law firm in Cheboygan, Michigan.

narrative_blocks.header: Horse shot at the Wilderness; wounded at Trevilian
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Sherman Humphrey served as a private in Company I, 1st Michigan Cavalry, enlisting December 11, 1863, and saw fierce action: his horse was shot under him at the Battle of the Wilderness in May 1864, and he was wounded at the Battle of Trevilian Station in June 1864 — two of the bloodiest cavalry engagements of the Overland Campaign — before being admitted to Harper's Hospital in Detroit on January 2, 1865. He was honorably discharged May 18, 1865, and afterward studied law under Kilbourne in Lansing before graduating from the University of Michigan in 1869.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded a Cheboygan law firm; ran for Congress
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Sherman Humphrey arrived in Cheboygan, Michigan in 1869 with his University of Michigan law degree and built one of the region's most prominent practices, founding Humphrey & Perks and serving as corporate counsel to Michigan Sugar and railroad interests; he was elected county treasurer in 1880 and in 1890 ran as the Republican candidate for Congress. His corporate reach extended to directorships at Mistee Lumber and Phelps Dodge — one of the nation's largest copper mining companies — making him a significant figure in the industrial development of northern Michigan.

narrative_blocks.header: A corporate lawyer for Michigan's mines and railroads
narrative_blocks.body: From his Cheboygan law practice, Watts Sherman Humphrey built his corporate reach to become a director of Phelps Dodge Corporation — then one of the largest copper mining companies in the United States, with mines in Arizona and New Mexico — and of Mistee Lumber, wiring together the extractive industrial economy of northern Michigan through legal work for railroads and mining interests that made him a significant figure in the region's development. His connections to natural resources and corporate law directly presaged his son George Magoffin Humphrey's career as president of M.A. Hanna Company and ultimately Secretary of the Treasury — two generations of men who ran the industrial sinew of American capitalism before entering public life.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin (1820-1883), Episcopal priest

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors his mother Mary Chaffee Hooker's maiden name — daughter of Captain James Hooker of Windsor, CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker to honor his mother's maiden name
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin carried 'Hooker' as his middle name to honor his mother Mary Chaffee Hooker, and passed the tradition forward — his son Frank was named 'Frank Hooker Magoffin,' making three consecutive generations of the Magoffin family to carry 'Hooker' as a given name. His grandson George Magoffin Humphrey became the 55th Secretary of the Treasury under President Eisenhower, carrying the heritage in the form of the middle name 'Magoffin.'

narrative_blocks.header: An Episcopal priest in a Presbyterian father's household
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend James Hooker Magoffin was ordained as an Episcopal priest despite being raised in a Presbyterian household — his father had been an elder in the Presbyterian Church in Schenectady — making him one of the quiet denominational crossovers of mid-nineteenth-century American religion, a son who took his faith in a different direction while preserving his mother's Hooker name in every way he could. He moved west from New York through Wisconsin, where his son Frank Hooker Magoffin was born in Waukesha in 1859, to Cheboygan County, Michigan, dying there in 1883 at sixty-three — following the American frontier even as an Episcopal clergyman.

burial.plot_notes: Forest Hill Cemetery Ann Arbor per FA
